title: Engineering superconductivity on the surface of Weyl semimetals
abstract: Ten years after the experimental discovery of Weyl semimetals, theoretical and experimental work has pointed to the possibility of realizing surface-only superconductivity at relatively high temperatures in these materials. A consensus is developing that this unusual form of superconductivity is mediated by surface electronic states unique to Weyl semimetals, known as Fermi arcs. In this work, we show that the topological protection of these exotic states can be exploited to engineer high critical temperatures. Motivated by a real-material example (PtBi$_2$), we demonstrate that surface van Hove singularities can be induced by depositing a suitable additional layer on top of the Weyl surface. We also investigate the role of these singularities in raising the critical temperature, showing that it is significantly enhanced when the chemical potential lies in their vicinity. More generally, our results demonstrate how topological protection can be exploited to manipulate surface electronic states, thereby opening experimentally accessible routes toward engineering high-temperature two-dimensional superconductivity and other exotic phases.
summary: This paper explores ways to engineer high-temperature superconductivity on the surfaces of Weyl semimetals. By adding a layer to the surface, the researchers show it is possible to create van Hove singularities that boost the critical temperature. The work highlights how the unique topological properties of Fermi arcs can be manipulated to create stable, high-temperature 2D superconducting phases. This provides a potential roadmap for designing new functional materials.
main_problem: How can the topological protection of surface states in Weyl semimetals be utilized to engineer and enhance high-temperature surface superconductivity?
main_result: The authors demonstrate that depositing an additional layer on a Weyl semimetal surface can induce van Hove singularities, which significantly enhance the superconducting critical temperature when the chemical potential is tuned to these singularities.
method: Theoretical investigation using a real-material model (PtBi2) to study the effect of surface engineering and chemical potential tuning on superconductivity.
model_or_system: The system consists of a Weyl semimetal surface characterized by Fermi arc electronic states, specifically using PtBi2 as a motivating example, with an additional layer deposited on top to manipulate surface electronic states.
key_observables: Superconducting critical temperature (Tc) and the presence of surface van Hove singularities.
important_parameters: Chemical potential position relative to van Hove singularities and the presence of an additional surface layer.
main_assumptions: The study assumes the existence of surface-only superconductivity mediated by Fermi arcs and focuses on the impact of induced van Hove singularities.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of surface superconductivity in Weyl semimetals, presents a model for inducing van Hove singularities via surface layering (using PtBi2 as a case study), analyzes the enhancement of Tc near these singularities, and concludes with the broader implications for engineering exotic 2D phases.
